articles.headline: Before Black Bohemia: Edmonia Lewis in the Post-Bellum, Pre-Harlem Period (dissertation)
articles.author: Melissa M. Benbow Flowers
articles.summary: University of Delaware PhD dissertation in English (Summer 2024, committee: John Ernest, Laura Helton, Emily Davis, Kimberly Blockett, Julie McGee) examining sculptor Edmonia Lewis (Mary Edmonia Lewis, c.1844-1907), of mixed Mississauga/Chippewa and African American (Haitian-descended) heritage, as a figure of Black print culture and commemoration practice bridging the post-Bellum and pre-Harlem periods. Covers her self-authored pamphlet 'How Edmonia Lewis Became an Artist,' her education at New York Central College (McGrawville, 1856-1858) and Oberlin College (1858-1863, including the 1862 poisoning accusation and vigilante assault and 1863 art-supply-theft expulsion), her Boston studio apprenticeship under Edward Brackett and abolitionist patronage circle, her Atlantic travel and Rome expatriate sculptor career, her exhibition at the 1876 Centennial International Exhibition (Death of Cleopatra) and campaign for inclusion at the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition, and her ongoing commemoration by Black press, clubwomen, and the Colored Conventions movement.

articles.headline: How Edmonia Lewis Became an Artist -- pamphlet and Charles Sumner's 1870 Harvard donation
articles.summary: Discussion of the autobiographical pamphlet 'How Edmonia Lewis Became an Artist,' donated by Senator Charles Sumner to Harvard's Houghton Library on November 1, 1870, alongside works by Frances Ellen Watkins Harper, Harriet Wilson, and Emma Dunham Kelley -- situating Lewis within a lineage of 19th-century Black women's print production.

articles.headline: Edmonia Lewis interviewed at The Revolution office by Laura Curtis Bullard
articles.summary: In 1869, shortly after returning to America, Lewis visited the office of The Revolution, the women's rights magazine owned by Susan B. Anthony and edited by Elizabeth Cady Stanton; contributor Laura Curtis Bullard interviewed her (published April 1871), material later drawn on by William Wells Brown's The Rising Son.

articles.headline: Henry Wreford's 1866 Athenaeum profile 'A Negro Sculptress'
articles.author: Henry Wreford
articles.summary: London Athenaeum correspondent Henry Wreford interviewed Lewis in Rome about her schooling and background; published as 'A Negro Sculptress,' March 3, 1866 (reprinted in full as Appendix D of the dissertation).

articles.headline: Marquis of Bute patronage and Sumner's Rome/Harvard social network
articles.summary: News spread in 1870 (Boston Daily Advertiser, May 21, 1870) that the European aristocrat Marquis of Bute had become one of Lewis's patrons. Discussion also traces the social network linking Sumner, Justice Joseph Story, sculptor William Wetmore Story (who moved to Rome in 1846), and poet Henry Wadsworth Longfellow, who sat for a Lewis marble bust in Rome in early 1868.

articles.headline: Longfellow bust subscription and Hagar exhibition at Farwell Hall, Chicago
articles.summary: By July 1869 (Christian Recorder, ed. Elisha Weaver) a $700 subscription campaign shipped Lewis's completed Longfellow bust to Harvard (arrived by 1871). Separately, her sculpture 'Hagar' (completed 1868) was exhibited at Farwell Hall, Chicago in 1870, reviewed favorably by the Chicago Tribune and New National Era; while in Chicago Lewis sat for carte-de-visite photographs at photographer Henry Rocher's studio.

articles.headline: Lewis' parentage: Ojibwe/Mississauga mother and Haitian-descended Black father
articles.summary: Discussion of Lewis's contested/shifting accounts of her parentage across interviews (Child 1864, Wreford 1866, Daily Graphic 1873, Indianapolis News 1878), her upbringing partly among her mother's Native family (basket-making, moccasin- and bead-work sold at Niagara Falls, Genesee Falls, and Watkins Glen), and a rumor (denied by Lewis via the Pacific Appeal) that her father was 'Robert of Bath, Maine' (San Jose Mercury-News, Oct. 11, 1873).

articles.headline: The Benjamin Franklin statue origin story
articles.summary: Lewis's oft-repeated origin story: encountering Richard S. Greenough and Thomas Ball's 1856 statue of Benjamin Franklin on School Street, Boston (dedicated Sept. 17, 1856, funded by $20,000 public subscription) inspired her to become a sculptor. Discussed via the pamphlet excerpt and Lydia Maria Child's 1869 National Anti-Slavery Standard account (Appendix B/C).

articles.headline: New York Central College (McGrawville): Lewis's first school
articles.summary: Lewis attended the racially-integrated New York Central College in McGraw/McGrawville, NY (1856-1858), founded 1848 by the American Free Baptist Mission Society. Manual Labor Department overseen by Luther Wellington; principal teacher Keziah King ran an 1853 educational experiment on Black/white student parity. Donors and fundraisers included William Lloyd Garrison, Henry Ward Beecher, Wendell Phillips, Gerrit Smith, and Horace Greeley; Charles Reason (first Black professor at a predominantly white American college) taught there; Frederick Douglass gave the 'Self-Made Men' address there in summer 1858, when Lewis (about 14) was a student.

articles.headline: Edmonia Lewis at Oberlin College, 1858-1863
articles.summary: Lewis attended Oberlin College's Young Ladies' Course 1860-1863 (preceded by preparatory study from 1858), boarding in the home of Rev. John Keep (trustee, cast the deciding vote admitting Black students to Oberlin) and his wife Lydia Hale/Keep, alongside eleven other young women. In early 1862 two housemates accused Lewis of poisoning them; vigilantes assaulted her; her lawyer John Mercer Langston (nearly shot while gathering defense evidence) won her acquittal. In February 1863 an art instructor accused her of stealing art supplies, and Ladies' Department principal Marianne Dascomb barred her from registering for her final semester, ending her Oberlin education without a degree. Other Oberlin-connected figures discussed: Owen Brown (trustee 1835-1844, father of John Brown), Professor James Monroe (namesake of the student militia 'Monroe Rifles'), Mary Jane Patterson (first African American woman to earn a B.A., Oberlin 1862; husband Lewis Sheridan Leary killed at Harpers Ferry), Mary Church Terrell and Anna Julia Cooper (later Oberlin graduates and Black feminist writers who invoked Lewis).

articles.headline: Boston abolitionist patronage: Garrison introduces Lewis to sculptor Edward Brackett
articles.summary: On arriving in Boston, Lewis was introduced by William Lloyd Garrison to sculptor Edward Brackett, who trained her. Garrison connected Lewis to his abolitionist circle including Wendell Phillips, Lydia Maria Child, and the Waterstons. Lewis produced portrait medallions/busts of Garrison, Phillips, Charles Sumner, Maria Weston Chapman, James McCune Smith, and John Brown to fund her 1865 move to Rome.

articles.headline: Sarah Parker Remond, the 1853 Howard Athenaeum incident, and the Howard family of Boston
articles.summary: Black abolitionist Sarah Parker Remond (who later moved to Rome, becoming an expatriate like Lewis) was involved, with her sister Caroline E. Putnam and activist William C. Nell, in an 1853 segregation incident at Boston's Howard Athenaeum. Separately, Lewis boarded with the Howard family of Boston: Edwin F. Howard (hairdresser) and wife Joanna, daughters Joan Imogene Howard (later a Board of Woman Managers of New York member for the 1893 Columbian Exposition, loaned Lewis's Hiawatha for the Woman's Building library) and Addie/Ada Howard (taught alongside Lewis in Richmond under the Freedmen's Bureau, summer 1865); also Edwin's relative Alfred G. Howard's household.

articles.headline: 1869 Festival of the Teachers of the Freedmen's Union Commission
articles.summary: July 1, 1869, Parker Fraternity Hall, Boston: Festival of the Teachers of the Freedmen's Union Commission, attended by William Lloyd Garrison, John G. Whittier, Rev. J.T. Sargent, Rev. Samuel J. May of Syracuse, Rev. John F.W. Ware of Baltimore, and Edmonia Lewis, commemorating Lewis's and Ada Howard's Freedmen's Bureau teaching service.

articles.headline: James Peck Thomas and Madame Pelagie Rutgers commissions, St. Louis
articles.summary: James Peck Thomas, a formerly enslaved, wealthy African American of St. Louis (mother Sally Thomas orchestrated his freedom; wife Antoinette Rutgers), hosted Lewis for a November 1873 interview (St. Louis Globe correspondent) and commissioned a bust of himself and a memorial statue for his mother-in-law, the wealthy Haitian Madame Pelagie Rutgers.

articles.headline: Travel segregation: Cleveland and Syracuse hotel discrimination
articles.summary: In 1869 Lewis was denied full hotel accommodation in Cleveland (Kennard House, then Forest City House) and denied admittance to a hotel in Syracuse, where she instead stayed with Rev. Jermain Wesley Loguen (Underground Railroad figure, AME minister) and his wife Caroline; Frederick Douglass's sons Lewis Henry Douglass (married Loguen's daughter Helen Amelia Loguen) and Charles Remond Douglass were connected to the Loguen family.

articles.headline: Presentation of 'Forever Free' to Rev. Leonard Grimes, Tremont Temple, Boston
articles.summary: October 18, 1869, Tremont Temple, Boston: Lewis presented her marble group 'Forever Free' (originally intended for Garrison) and a portrait bust to Rev. Leonard Andrew Grimes of the Twelfth Baptist Church ('the Fugitive Slave Church'), commemorating his Underground Railroad activism. Speakers included Rev. Justin Dewey Fulton, Rev. Robert Cassie Waterston, William Lloyd Garrison, William Craft (with wife Ellen Craft, co-authors of 'Running a Thousand Miles for Freedom'), and William Wells Brown; Mrs. Miller sang.

articles.headline: Edmonia Lewis's Boston farewell reception, Phillips Street Baptist Church
articles.summary: November 1869, Phillips Street Baptist Church, Boston: farewell reception for Lewis before her return to Rome, presided over by George L. Ruffin, with remarks from Rev. Leonard Grimes, William Craft, and J.B. Smith; Lewis was presented a purse of money and an ivory mallet.

articles.headline: Lewis's 1873 San Francisco exhibition, Art Association Rooms
articles.summary: September 1, 1873, 313 Pine Street, San Francisco: exhibition of five Lewis sculptures at the San Francisco Art Association (founded 1871 at the home of Juan Buckingham Wandesforde) -- Hiawatha's Marriage (sold $550 to Mrs. C.L. Low), a Lincoln bust (sold $550 via San Jose Library subscription), 'Asleep' and 'Awake' (sold to Mrs. Knox of San Jose), and 'Love in a Trap' (sold $500 to Frank M. Pixley). A reception followed at Third Baptist Church, Powell Street (San Francisco's first Black congregation, founded 1852), hosted by a committee of ladies and gentlemen. Coverage by the Black papers The Elevator (Philip A. Bell) and Pacific Appeal (Peter Anderson, M.W. Gibbs).

articles.headline: 1869 Colored National Labor Convention names Lewis in Women's Labor report
articles.summary: December 6-10, 1869, Washington, D.C.: John Mercer Langston addressed the Colored National Labor Convention ('The Relations of the Colored People to American Industry'); the Committee on Women's Labor report cited Edmonia Lewis's professional 'aptitude and ability' alongside Mrs. S.M. Douglass (physician), Miss Cole (physician), and Miss Ketchum (clerk).

articles.headline: Frederick Douglass's 'Miss Edmonia Lewis' (New National Era, 1873)
articles.author: Frederick Douglass
articles.summary: September 25, 1873, New National Era: Douglass recalls first meeting Lewis c. spring 1863 while she was a student at Oberlin; acknowledges both the 'advantages' of her mixed heritage in Italy/Boston and the 'discouragements' from her own countrymen; written partly in response to public attention Lewis received amid comparisons to the Vinnie Ream Lincoln-bust controversy.

articles.headline: First Convention of Colored Newspaper Men, Cincinnati, August 1875
articles.summary: August 1875, Union Chapel (morning) and Allen Temple (evening), Cincinnati: the first 'Convention of Colored Newspaper Men,' chaired by P.B.S. Pinchback (Louisiana Lt. Gov., editor of the Louisianian/Weekly Louisianan), with delegates including George Thompson Ruby (Galveston Spectator), Louis H. Douglass (The Elevator), M.W. Gibbs (Pacific Appeal), Bishop Benjamin William Arnett and Rev. Henry McNeal Turner (Christian Recorder). Peter H. Clark made opening remarks and was elected president of the Committee on Organization; Lewis H. Douglass elected vice president; J. Sella Martin spoke on convention scope. Correspondence was read from Frederick Douglass, Peter Anderson, and Bishop Daniel A. Payne.

articles.headline: Resolution for the Colored Women of America commissions Lewis's Centennial artwork
articles.summary: At the 1875 Cincinnati Convention of Colored Newspaper Men, the Committee on Centennial Celebration (Bishop Benjamin W. Arnett, P.B.S. Pinchback, Rev. Henry McNeal Turner) proposed, and delegates unanimously adopted, a resolution commissioning a 'statue, or work of art' from Edmonia Lewis 'in the name of the colored women of America' for the 1876 Centennial, with plans to later place it in a Washington D.C. public building or park and to form a ladies' Centennial Association to raise funds.

articles.headline: Richard Allen Monument fundraising campaign, Fairmount Park
articles.summary: 1874-1876 fundraising campaign led by Bishop Daniel A. Payne and the AME Church for a bronze monument to Bishop Richard Allen in Fairmount Park, Philadelphia, repeatedly postponed (planned July 4 1876, rescheduled Sept. 22 1876, then further delayed after the base was reportedly lost in the Susquehanna River, rescheduled November 1876). Indiana fundraising committee members named: Wm. H. Howard, John B. Lott, Alfred Carter, Alexander Moss, John Carter, Henry Stepp, W.J. Sismore, J.S. Hinton.

articles.headline: Centennial International Exhibition of 1876, Philadelphia -- Death of Cleopatra exhibited
articles.summary: Opened May 10, 1876, Fairmount Park, Philadelphia. Edmonia Lewis exhibited 'Death of Cleopatra,' the only artwork by an African American artist at the Centennial (the only other Black-themed exhibit, 'The Freed Slave,' was not by a Black artist). Frederick Douglass was seated as a dignitary but initially barred from the speakers' platform until Senator Roscoe Conkling intervened; Judge William D. Kelly unsuccessfully urged that Douglass be allowed to read the Emancipation Proclamation. John P. Sampson (founder of the Cincinnati Colored Citizen) visited and later published 'Doing the Centennial' (Christian Recorder, Oct. 19, 1876), quoting Lewis's frustration at criticism from fellow African Americans. Lewis lodged during the Centennial at Mrs. Sarah Young's boarding house (1836 Lombard St.) and the Keystone Centennial House (proprietress Mrs. C.E. Gilbert, 625/627 Pine St.), the latter also hosting Bishop Arnett and named guests Mrs. Julia F. Reed, Mrs. Delia N. Butler, Mr. John R. Parker, Mr. & Mrs. J.D. Bowser, Miss Anna M. Bowser, Mr. Thomas Christer, and Rev. Thomas Simpson. During the Centennial, Lewis Hayden presented a historical paper to the Colored Ladies Centennial Club, and John Mercer Langston delivered a historical oration to the Banneker Lyceum.

articles.headline: World's Columbian Exposition of 1893, Chicago -- petitioning for Black inclusion
articles.summary: Chicago, 1893. Rev. Henry McNeal Turner personally petitioned three times in person for Black representation at the Fair. 'Colored American Day' was designated August 25, 1893, with Frederick Douglass as keynote speaker. Henry Ossawa Tanner's painting was exhibited only via the French exhibit. Ida B. Wells published 'The Reason Why the Colored American Is Not in the World's Columbian Exposition' (Aug. 30, 1893, essay 'The Art of Sculpture' by I. Garland Penn briefly discussed Lewis, excluding Cleopatra), urging a Black boycott of the fair. Separately, an Allegheny County (Pittsburgh) Black women's planning committee (member Ms. McCandless) corresponded with Lewis (letter dated Jan. 10, 1893; Lewis's response published Detroit Plaindealer, March 17, 1893) seeking to commission a Phillis Wheatley bust, which did not arrive in time; instead Joan Imogene Howard, on the Board of Woman Managers of New York (Committee on Education), loaned a copy of Lewis's 'Hiawatha' for the Woman's Building library.

articles.headline: George Washington Williams's 1876 oration names Lewis among 'representative' African Americans
articles.author: Rev. George Washington Williams
articles.summary: July 4, 1876, Avondale, Cincinnati: Rev. George Washington Williams (later author of History of the Negro Race in America, 1619-1880) delivered 'The American Negro from 1776 to 1876,' naming Edmonia Lewis alongside James McCune Smith, James M. Whitfield, James W.C. Pennington, Alexander Crummell, David Ruggles, Robert Purvis, William C. Nell, J. Madison Bell, Charlotte Forten, Frances E. Harper, and Fanny Jackson as representative African Americans.

articles.headline: Founding of the American Negro Academy, 1897
articles.summary: 1897, Metropolitan AME Church, Washington D.C.: Alexander Crummell founded the American Negro Academy, 'of authors, scholars, artists...for the promotion of Letters, Science, and Art,' with members including W.E.B. Du Bois, Booker T. Washington, Paul Laurence Dunbar, James Weldon Johnson, Carter G. Woodson, Alain Locke, Arthur Schomburg, and Archibald H. Grimke -- part of the Black intelligentsia's commemoration practice that carried forward Lewis's legacy into the New Negro era.

articles.headline: Appendix D: Henry Wreford, 'A Negro Sculptress,' The Athenaeum, March 3, 1866
articles.author: Henry Wreford
articles.summary: Full reprint of Wreford's Athenaeum profile describing Lewis's appearance, manner, and background as observed in her Rome studio.

articles.headline: Appendix E: 'Sculpture in Rome. The Works of American Ladies' (American Art Journal, 1866)
articles.summary: 1866 profile naming Lewis alongside fellow American sculptor Harriet Hosmer ('From the same country as Miss Hosmer, is Miss Edmonia Lewis, a coloured girl...') as members of the American sculptors' community in Rome.

events.name: New York Central College (McGrawville)
events.description: Racially integrated Manual Labor College founded 1848 by the American Free Baptist Mission Society. Edmonia Lewis attended 1856-1858. Manual Labor Department overseen by Luther Wellington; principal teacher Keziah King ran a notable 1853 educational experiment on Black/white student parity; Charles Reason (first African American professor at a predominantly white American college) taught mathematics, Greek, Latin, French, and belles-lettres there until 1852. Frederick Douglass delivered the 'Self-Made Men' address to the college's literary society in summer 1858, while Lewis (about 14) was a student.

events.name: Oberlin College
events.description: Edmonia Lewis attended Oberlin College (preparatory study from 1858, Young Ladies' Course 1860-1863), boarding in the home of trustee Rev. John Keep and his wife Lydia Hale/Keep alongside eleven other young women. Notable Oberlin-connected figures during or around her attendance: Owen Brown (trustee 1835-1844, father of John Brown), Professor James Monroe (namesake of student militia 'Monroe Rifles'), Mary Jane Patterson (first African American woman to earn a Oberlin B.A., 1862), Marianne (M.P.) Dascomb (principal, Ladies' Department, barred Lewis's final-semester registration in 1863), Mary Church Terrell and Anna Julia Cooper (Oberlin graduates and Black feminist writers who later invoked Lewis's legacy), and John Mercer Langston (Oberlin alumnus and Lewis's lawyer). In early 1862 Lewis was accused of poisoning two housemates and was assaulted by vigilantes; Langston (nearly shot while gathering defense evidence) won her acquittal. In February 1863 an unnamed art instructor accused her of stealing art supplies, leading to her effective expulsion.
attendees: trustee; boarding-house host; cast deciding vote to admit Black students
student, first Black woman to earn a B.A. at Oberlin (1862)
principal, Ladies' Department; barred Lewis's final-semester registration
later Oberlin graduate; Black feminist writer on Lewis
later Oberlin graduate; Black feminist writer on Lewis
Oberlin alumnus; Lewis's defense lawyer in the 1862 poisoning accusation

events.name: 1862 Oberlin poisoning accusation, vigilante assault, and trial
events.description: Two of Lewis's housemates in the John Keep household accused her of poisoning them; she was subsequently badly assaulted by a vigilante mob. Her lawyer, John Mercer Langston, was nearly shot while gathering evidence for her defense but won her acquittal. The dissertation notes that the accusers, victims, and vigilantes are not named in its sources (which cite Henderson & Henderson's The Indomitable Spirit of Edmonia Lewis and Geoffrey Blodgett's 1968 Journal of Negro History article 'John Mercer Langston and the Case of Edmonia Lewis: Oberlin, 1862' for fuller detail).
attendees: defense lawyer; nearly shot gathering evidence; won acquittal
boarding-house host in whose home the accusation arose

events.name: 1863 Oberlin art-supply theft accusation and expulsion
events.description: An unnamed art instructor accused Lewis of stealing art supplies in February 1863; despite insufficient evidence, Ladies' Department principal Marianne Dascomb barred Lewis from registering for her final semester, ending her Oberlin education without a degree.

events.name: 1870 Chicago assault during Hagar exhibition
events.description: While exhibiting 'Hagar in the Wilderness' in Chicago in 1870, Lewis was assaulted by an unnamed man who did not believe an African and Native American woman could have created the sculpture herself (per Buffalo Courier Express, Sept. 17, 1878). The assailant is not named in the dissertation's sources.

events.name: Boston studio apprenticeship under Edward Brackett
events.description: On arriving in Boston after leaving Oberlin, Lewis was introduced by William Lloyd Garrison to sculptor Edward Brackett, who trained her. She produced portrait medallions/busts of Garrison, Wendell Phillips, Charles Sumner, Maria Weston Chapman, James McCune Smith, and John Brown, in part to fund her 1865 relocation to Rome.

events.name: Rome expatriate sculptor colony
events.description: Lewis relocated to Rome in 1865 and became part of the American expatriate artists' community there (later dubbed by scholars the 'White Marmorean Flock'), which included sculptors William Wetmore Story (moved to Rome 1846, son of Justice Joseph Story, close friend of Charles Sumner), Harriet Hosmer, and Anne Whitney, plus fellow Black expatriate Sarah Parker Remond (moved to Rome 1858). Henry Wadsworth Longfellow sat for a Lewis bust in her Rome studio in early 1868; London Athenaeum correspondent Henry Wreford interviewed her there in 1866.
attendees: sculptor; expatriate community member; wrote critically of Lewis in private correspondence
Black abolitionist expatriate, moved to Rome 1858

events.name: 1853 Howard Athenaeum segregation incident
events.description: Segregation incident at Boston's Howard Athenaeum theater involving Black abolitionist Sarah Parker Remond, her sister Caroline E. Putnam, and activist William C. Nell -- discussed in the dissertation's Boston chapter as context for the racial climate Lewis navigated as a Black woman in 1860s Boston.

events.name: The Howard family of Boston
events.description: Lewis boarded with the Howard family of Boston during her studio-training years: Edwin F. Howard (hairdresser, age 51) and wife Joanna (age 39); daughters Joan Imogene Howard (age 16 in 1860, teacher, later Board of Woman Managers of New York for the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition, loaned Lewis's 'Hiawatha' for the Woman's Building library, died 1937) and Addie/Ada Howard (age 20 in 1860, teacher, taught alongside Lewis in Richmond under the Freedmen's Bureau summer 1865, died 1922 Philadelphia); also connected: Alfred G. Howard (relative, hairdresser) and wife Martha, their daughter Ada Howard (age 12), domestic servant Julia Coleman, and seamstress Jane R. Killings.
attendees: daughter; teacher; later 1893 World's Fair Board of Woman Managers (NY)
daughter; teacher; taught with Lewis in Richmond, 1865

events.name: Festival of the Teachers of the Freedmen's Union Commission
events.description: Festival honoring teachers of the Freedmen's Union Commission (including Lewis and Ada Howard, who had taught freedpeople in Richmond in 1865).

events.name: Presentation of Forever Free to Rev. Leonard Grimes
events.description: Lewis presented her marble group 'Forever Free' (originally intended for Garrison) and a portrait bust to Rev. Leonard Andrew Grimes of the Twelfth Baptist Church ('the Fugitive Slave Church'), commemorating his Underground Railroad activism.

events.name: Edmonia Lewis's Boston farewell reception
events.location: Phillips Street Baptist Church, Boston, Massachusetts
events.description: Farewell reception for Lewis before her return to Rome; she was presented a purse of money and an ivory mallet.

events.name: Lewis's 1873 San Francisco exhibition, Art Association Rooms
events.description: Exhibition of five Lewis sculptures at the San Francisco Art Association: Hiawatha's Marriage (sold to Mrs. C.L. Low), a Lincoln bust (sold via San Jose Library subscription), 'Asleep' and 'Awake' (sold to Mrs. Knox of San Jose), and 'Love in a Trap' (sold to Frank M. Pixley). Followed by a reception at Third Baptist Church, Powell Street.
attendees: editor, The Elevator, covered the exhibition
editor, Pacific Appeal, covered the exhibition

events.name: 1869 Colored National Labor Convention
events.description: John Mercer Langston addressed the convention ('The Relations of the Colored People to American Industry'); the Committee on Women's Labor report cited Edmonia Lewis's professional achievement alongside other Black professional women.
attendees: cited in Women's Labor Committee report

events.name: First Convention of Colored Newspaper Men
events.location: Union Chapel and Allen Temple, Cincinnati, Ohio
events.description: First national convention of Black newspaper editors, chaired by P.B.S. Pinchback; produced the resolution commissioning Lewis's Centennial artwork 'in the name of the colored women of America.'
attendees: delegate, The Elevator; elected vice president, Committee on Organization
delegate, Christian Recorder; Committee on Centennial Celebration
delegate, Christian Recorder; Committee on Centennial Celebration
opening remarks; elected president, Committee on Organization; Lewis's patron

events.name: Resolution for the Colored Women of America
events.description: Resolution adopted at the 1875 Convention of Colored Newspaper Men commissioning Edmonia Lewis to create a work of art for the 1876 Centennial 'in the name of the colored women of America.'
attendees: Committee on Centennial Celebration; spoke in favor

events.name: Richard Allen Monument fundraising campaign
events.description: AME Church-led fundraising campaign for a bronze monument to Bishop Richard Allen, repeatedly postponed; Indiana state fundraising committee named.

events.name: Centennial International Exhibition of 1876
events.description: Lewis exhibited 'Death of Cleopatra,' the only artwork by an African American at the Centennial. Frederick Douglass was seated as a dignitary but barred from the speakers' platform until Senator Roscoe Conkling intervened; Judge William D. Kelly unsuccessfully urged that Douglass be allowed to read the Emancipation Proclamation. Related commemorative events during the Centennial included Lewis Hayden's historical paper to the Colored Ladies Centennial Club and John Mercer Langston's oration to the Banneker Lyceum.
attendees: attendee, barred from platform then admitted
Judge; advocated for Douglass to read Emancipation Proclamation
presented historical paper, Colored Ladies Centennial Club
Bishop, Centennial visitor, stayed at Gilbert's boarding house
Mrs., Centennial visitor, Washington Heights NY
Mr. & Mrs., Centennial visitors, Kansas City

events.name: World's Columbian Exposition of 1893
events.description: Rev. Henry McNeal Turner petitioned three times in person for Black representation. 'Colored American Day' (Aug 25, 1893) featured Frederick Douglass as keynote speaker. Ida B. Wells published a protest pamphlet urging a Black boycott; an Allegheny County Black women's committee (Ms. McCandless) sought to commission a Phillis Wheatley bust from Lewis; Joan Imogene Howard (Board of Woman Managers of New York, Committee on Education) loaned Lewis's 'Hiawatha' for the Woman's Building library instead.
attendees: author of protest pamphlet; boycott organizer
author, 'The Art of Sculpture' essay discussing Lewis
painter, exhibited via the French exhibit
Allegheny County women's committee; corresponded with Lewis
Board of Woman Managers of New York, Committee on Education; loaned Lewis's Hiawatha
sought-after sculptor; work exhibited via loan

events.name: American Negro Academy
events.description: Founded by Alexander Crummell as a learned society 'of authors, scholars, artists...for the promotion of Letters, Science, and Art,' part of the Black intelligentsia's commemoration practice carrying Lewis's legacy forward.

agents.note: 19th/early-20th century authors who included Edmonia Lewis biographical sketches in books documenting notable African Americans, tracing the spread of her story through Black print culture
names: author, History of the Negro Race in America 1619-1880 (1882)
author, Negro Neighbors, Bond and Free (1907)
author, The Story of the Negro (1909)
co-author, The Industrial History of the Negro Race (1911)
co-author, The Industrial History of the Negro Race (1911)
author, The Colored American from Slavery to Honorable Citizenship (1912)

agents.note: Modern art historians and biographers whose scholarship this dissertation builds on for reconstructing Edmonia Lewis's career (not contemporaries of Lewis; included for completeness of the scholarly record, tagged distinctly from 19th-century figures)
names: modern scholar; author, Child of the Fire (2010)
modern scholar; author, The Color of Stone (2007)
modern scholar; author, Race and Racism in Nineteenth-Century Art (2021)
modern scholar; author, 'Edmonia Lewis at McGrawville' (2000)
modern scholar; author on Death of Cleopatra
modern scholar; author on Lewis's Minnehaha
modern scholar; dissertation on Lewis's dual heritage (1995)

agents.note: Additional named Black newspaper editors/journalists of the era discussed as part of the Black press network that commemorated Lewis
names: editor, Freedom's Journal; mentor to Philip A. Bell
editor, Daily Graphic; interviewed Lewis July 10, 1873
editor, Christian Recorder 1868-1884; father of Henry Ossawa Tanner

agents.note: Black U.S. congressmen named alongside the Colored Newspaper Men convention discussion of Reconstruction-era political gains
